APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes. The paper addresses an NLP research problem (causal relation/AltLex disambiguation) using neural methods (LSTM and pre-trained GloVe embeddings) and standard evaluation against the prior SVM-based state-of-the-art (Hidey & McKeown, 2016), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The task formulation (disambiguation/classification of causal lexical markers), experimental setup (use of AltLex corpus, baselines B1/B2, optimizer choices), and related-work positioning demonstrate it targets the right audience and uses methods/evaluation standards expected at such conferences.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a broadly logical sectioning (intro, method, experiments, conclusion) but the provided text contains heavy duplication and repetition (e.g. multiple verbatim repeats of Bethard and Martin (2008) paragraphs and several reuse of identical sentences), which disrupts flow and makes it hard to follow. The main contribution (a neural network architecture for causality disambiguation) is stated, but not prominently early and is buried among repeated material. Notation is minimal and consistent, but figures/tables are only referenced (Table 3, Table 8) and not shown in the excerpt, and examples/explanations are often awkward or grammatically strained (e.g. tense/wording issues and occasional awkward sentences). Overall the writing quality and presentation need substantial cleanup to remove duplicates, tighten organization, and fix grammar.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem — disambiguating causal lexical markers and thus improving causality classification (the authors state "Causality classification...the encoding of the meaning of a sentence is required"), which is central to event understanding and discourse parsing. It demonstrates a practical advance by using LSTM-based neural encoding with word embeddings to outperform the prior SVM/lexical-resources approach on the AltLex corpus ("we outperform the state-of-the-art" and comparison to Hidey and McKeown (2016)). However, impact is moderated by limited evaluation scope: the authors themselves note the field "lacks representative or large enough corpus" and that experiments are run only on the AltLex data, which constrains broad generalization and immediate widespread adoption.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims (neural/LSTM model using GloVe embeddings outperforms the SVM-based state-of-the-art on the AltLex corpus) and uses reasonable baselines (B1 most-frequent and B2 Hidey & McKeown 2016) as noted in Section 4.1 and elsewhere. However, the provided excerpt does not include quantitative results, evaluation numbers, dataset sizes (Table 3) or significance testing, and key experimental details (exact model architecture, train/test splits, hyperparameters) are not shown, so the central claim “we outperform the state-of-the-art” is not verifiable from the text. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and while qualitative examples (Table 8) are cited to support contextual encoding, the lack of reported metrics or statistical analyses introduces concerns about the completeness of the experimental evidence.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing neural techniques (LSTM RNNs and pre-trained word embeddings; see mentions of LSTM and GloVe) to the task of disambiguating causal lexical markers on the AltLex corpus (Hidey & McKeown, 2016). The problem itself (causality classification) is well-studied and the key novelty is framing it with neural context encoding rather than heavy feature engineering/SVM (the paper contrasts its approach with Hidey & McKeown and prior SVM/feature-based work). While the paper clearly differentiates its contribution and shows empirical gains on a specific corpus, the methods are a straightforward application of existing architectures rather than a new model or theoretical perspective, so the contribution is moderately incremental.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments compare against two baselines (majority baseline B1 and Hidey & McKeown 2016 B2) on both AltLex corpus versions (non‑bootstrapping and bootstrapping) and report model choices such as GloVe 300d embeddings and optimizers (Adadelta, Adam), and include qualitative error analysis with examples (Section 4, Analysis and references to Table 8). However, evaluation is confined to the AltLex corpus only (they even note it is the only corpus used), so generalization is limited, and there is no explicit ablation or sensitivity study of model components despite mentioning a “best configuration.” Reproducibility is partial—some choices (embeddings, optimizers, LSTM use) are given but full architectural/hyperparameter details and training settings are not present in the provided text.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a sensible set of baselines: a majority-class baseline (B1) and the Hidey & McKeown (2016) system (B2), which the authors correctly identify as the state-of-the-art on the AltLex corpus and evaluate on both corpus versions ( Sections 4 and 4.1 ). Comparisons appear fair in that they use the same dataset and present qualitative and quantitative claims of outperforming B2 (Section 4.3), and the paper discusses why their neural approach differs (use of embeddings and contextual encoding vs. lexical-resource-heavy SVM features). Minor shortcomings are that other recent causal-classification systems (e.g., Mirza & Tonelli) are discussed but not used as quantitative baselines, and the paper gives limited discussion of cases where prior methods might perform comparably or better.
